steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 2 argues that financial security shapes who becomes a poet because serious poetry publication requires professional training and time that full-time workers lack. The question asks how Lorde (Text 1) would respond — specifically, which of her claims would push back on Text 2's argument.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — By indicating that those poets who publish in major poetry journals are most likely to be able to earn a living by writing poetry.: Text 1 makes no claim about publication venues or income from poetry — Lorde's point is about when and where poetry can be written. ✗
  • Choice B — By pointing out that people can produce valuable poetry in other circumstances than those described by the author of Text 2.: Lorde's core claim is that poets can write "between shifts, in the hospital pantry, on the subway" and still make "worthwhile art" — directly countering Text 2's premise that time not available to full-time workers is required. ✓
  • Choice C — By suggesting that those artists who specialize in more financially rewarding artistic forms are unlikely to also be successful as poets.: Text 1 says nothing about artists in other fields trying poetry. This is out of scope. ✗
  • Choice D — By asserting that people often work full-time jobs in order to afford the professional training described in Text 2.: Text 1 never mentions professional training or working to fund it — this reverses the relationship and contradicts Lorde's actual point. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
